period_summary: Citations cluster heavily in the 4th century, particularly among Greek fathers engaged in Christological debates during the Arian controversy, revealing the passage's centrality to articulating Christ's divinity and eternal generation.

citations.cited_text: For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given, whose government is upon his shoulder, and his name is called Messenger of Great Counsel
citations.text_form_note: The title 'Messenger of Great Counsel' (ἄγγελος μεγάλης βουλῆς) is characteristic of the LXX Isaiah 9:6a and entirely absent from MT, which reads 'Wonderful Counselor' (פֶּלֶא יוֹעֵץ).
citations.theological_use: Justin uses this passage to demonstrate that Christ was prophesied as both messenger and divine counsel-bearer, arguing against Trypho that Jesus fulfills Isaiah's messianic prophecy as the preexistent divine agent.
citations.transmission_implication: Attests the early circulation of the LXX reading 'Messenger of Great Counsel' in mid-2nd century Christian scriptural usage at Rome.

citations.cited_text: unto us a child is born, and unto us a son is given... and his name shall be called Messenger of the Great Counsel, Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Eternal Father, Prince of Peace
citations.text_form_note: Irenaeus combines the LXX's 'Messenger of Great Counsel' with titles that reflect both LXX and something closer to MT ('Mighty God, Eternal Father'), creating a hybrid citation that draws from multiple traditions.
citations.theological_use: Irenaeus employs the passage to affirm both Christ's human birth and divine nature against Gnostic Christologies, emphasizing the unity of the incarnate Son with the eternal God.
citations.transmission_implication: Suggests fluidity in textual traditions in late 2nd-century Gaul, where Greek and possibly Old Latin forms circulated together.

citations.cited_text: For a child is born to us, a son is given to us, whose power is upon his shoulder, and his name is called Angel of Great Counsel
citations.text_form_note: Tertullian's Latin citation clearly reflects the LXX's 'Angel of Great Counsel' (Angelus magni consilii) via Old Latin translation, not the MT's distinct title structure.
citations.theological_use: Tertullian uses this text to distinguish the persons of the Trinity against modalism, arguing that the Son is the divine messenger-executor of the Father's eternal counsel.
citations.transmission_implication: Confirms that Old Latin versions in North Africa by c. 200 CE were based on LXX text forms, not Hebrew.

citations.cited_text: For a child is born to us, a son is given to us, and the government shall be upon his shoulder, and his name is called Messenger of Great Counsel, Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace
citations.text_form_note: Eusebius follows the LXX closely, including the distinctive 'Messenger of Great Counsel' first, though his full title list shows awareness of additional traditional throne names.
citations.theological_use: Eusebius interprets the passage as prophecy of Christ's dual role as divine messenger and eternal ruler, using it to demonstrate biblical proof of Christian claims against Jewish and pagan objections.
citations.transmission_implication: Demonstrates the dominance of LXX text forms in Palestinian Christian scholarship of the early 4th century.

citations.cited_text: For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given, and the government is upon his shoulder, and his name is called Angel of Great Counsel, Mighty God
citations.text_form_note: Athanasius cites the LXX reading 'Angel of Great Counsel' and 'Mighty God,' using these titles to argue for Christ's full divinity against Arian subordinationism.
citations.theological_use: Athanasius deploys this passage as biblical proof that the Son is 'Mighty God,' not a creature, emphasizing that the one born as child is eternally divine, countering Arian Christology.
citations.transmission_implication: Reflects the standard Alexandrian LXX text in mid-4th century anti-Arian polemics.

citations.cited_text: the Son who is born... whose name is Angel of Great Counsel and Mighty God and Prince of Peace and Father of the Age to Come
citations.text_form_note: Gregory uses the LXX's 'Angel of Great Counsel' and 'Father of the Age to Come' (πατὴρ τοῦ μέλλοντος αἰῶνος), a rendering distinct from MT's 'Everlasting Father.'
citations.theological_use: Gregory invokes these titles to articulate the paradox of the incarnation: the eternal Son becomes child, the Mighty God is born, defending Nicene orthodoxy against both Arians and Apollinarians.
citations.transmission_implication: Shows continued exclusive use of LXX text forms in Cappadocian theological discourse in the late 4th century.

citations.cited_text: For a child is born to us, a son is given to us, whose government is upon his shoulder, and his name shall be called Angel of Great Counsel, Wonderful, Counselor, Mighty God, Powerful, Prince of Peace, Father of the age to come
citations.text_form_note: Chrysostom follows the LXX closely with 'Angel of Great Counsel' and 'Father of the age to come,' though his citation shows slight expansion of the title list typical of liturgical or homiletical tradition.
citations.theological_use: Chrysostom emphasizes the passage as prophecy of the incarnation, stressing how titles of divinity ('Mighty God') and messianic rule ('Prince of Peace') unite in the child born in Bethlehem.
citations.transmission_implication: Confirms the LXX as the exclusive biblical text in Antiochene exegesis and preaching by the early 5th century.

citations.cited_text: For a child is born to us, a son is given to us, and the government shall be upon his shoulder, and his name is called Angel of Great Counsel
citations.text_form_note: Cyril's citation is a direct and careful rendering of the LXX, preserving the distinctive 'Angel of Great Counsel' that he then exposits at length in his commentary.
citations.theological_use: Cyril uses the passage to defend Chalcedonian Christology, arguing that the titles demonstrate the single divine person (hypostasis) who assumes human nature without confusion or division.
citations.transmission_implication: Represents the mature Alexandrian LXX textual tradition in mid-5th century, showing remarkable stability from earlier Alexandrian fathers.

transmission_synthesis: The patristic citation history of Isaiah 9:6 reveals near-total dominance of the LXX text form in Christian usage from the 2nd through 5th centuries, with the distinctive reading 'Angel/Messenger of Great Counsel' (ἄγγελος μεγάλης βουλῆς) appearing consistently across all geographic regions and linguistic traditions (Greek and Old Latin). Only one citation (Irenaeus) shows mixed characteristics, possibly indicating awareness of Hebrew or alternative Greek traditions, but even this remains primarily LXX-based. The uniformity is striking: no Church Father in this period cites the passage according to the MT's 'Wonderful Counselor' (פֶּלֶא יוֹעֵץ), suggesting that Christian biblical transmission was mediated exclusively through the LXX and its daughter versions until at least the mid-5th century. This pattern supports the thesis that the LXX functioned as the Christian Old Testament, with the Hebrew text playing virtually no role in textual transmission within ecclesiastical contexts.
transmission_synthesis_plain: Early Christian writers uniformly quoted Isaiah 9:6 from the Greek Septuagint, using the title 'Angel of Great Counsel' rather than the Hebrew Bible's 'Wonderful Counselor.' This shows that Christians transmitted the Old Testament through Greek translations, not Hebrew manuscripts, across all regions of the early church for at least four centuries.

notable_variants.reading: Angel/Messenger of Great Counsel (ἄγγελος μεγάλης βουλῆς)
notable_variants.significance: This distinctive LXX reading, completely absent from MT, demonstrates that the Septuagint served as the sole biblical text for Christian theology and liturgy. Its universal attestation in patristic sources suggests a stable, unified LXX textual tradition for this passage across centuries and regions.

notable_variants.reading: Father of the Age to Come (πατὴρ τοῦ μέλλοντος αἰῶνος)
notable_variants.significance: This LXX rendering differs from MT's 'Everlasting Father' (אֲבִיעַד) and was theologically significant for Greek fathers articulating the Son's role in establishing the eschatological kingdom, showing how translation differences shaped theological development.

notable_variants.reading: Hybrid title list combining LXX and additional traditional names
notable_variants.significance: Irenaeus's expanded citation may reflect liturgical or catechetical traditions that harmonized LXX readings with other known messianic titles, suggesting some textual fluidity in 2nd-century Gaul where multiple textual streams may have intersected.

bibcrit_assessment.title: LXX Monopoly in Christian Isaiah 9:6 Transmission
bibcrit_assessment.reasoning: The complete absence of MT-aligned citations across four centuries and all major ecclesiastical centers reveals that Christian biblical transmission operated independently of Jewish Hebrew textual traditions. The universal attestation of the LXX's distinctive 'Angel of Great Counsel' demonstrates not only preference but exclusive reliance on Greek Old Testament forms. This has profound implications: Christian theology of Christ's titles developed from a text form significantly different from the Hebrew, meaning Christological doctrine was shaped by translational choices made by Hellenistic Jewish translators centuries before Christ.
bibcrit_assessment.plain: Early Christians quoted this messianic prophecy exclusively from Greek translations, never from Hebrew. This means foundational Christian beliefs about Jesus' divine titles were actually based on how ancient Jewish translators interpreted Isaiah, not the original Hebrew words.
